---
title: "Gallberry (Ilex glabra)"
slug: "ilex-glabra"
group: "Plantae"
image: "https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/623648743/medium.jpg"        # keep for thumbnails
image-alt: "Gallberry"  # keep for a11y
tax_kingdom: "Plantae"
tax_class: "Magnoliopsida"
tax_order: "Aquifoliales"
tax_family: "Aquifoliaceae"
categories: ["Plantae", "Magnoliopsida", "Aquifoliales", "Aquifoliaceae"]   # these drive the chips + filtering
freeze: true
---

<figure class="species-figure">

<img src="https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/623648743/medium.jpg" alt="Gallberry" class="species-photo"/>

<figcaption class="photo-credit">

Photo: <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/people/garccyy">
garccyy</a> (CC BY-NC) via <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/342463263">iNaturalist</a>

</figcaption>

</figure>

## Taxonomy

-   **Class:** Magnoliopsida
-   **Order:** Aquifoliales
-   **Family:** Aquifoliaceae

## Occurrence at DeLuca Preserve

-   [DeLuca Bio Blitz Species Observations](https://www.inaturalist.org/observations?project_id=191727&subview=map&taxon_id=129347)

## Conservation & rarity context

-   IUCN: Not Listed
-   **Rarity note:** Common species in Florida found in moist to wet habitats.

## Natural history notes

An evergreen shrub characterized by its smooth, dark green leaves and small black berries. In Florida, it is commonly found in moist to wet habitats such as flatwoods, bogs, and swamp margins. It typically blooms in spring, and its flowers provide an important nectar source for pollinators, while the fruits are eaten by wildlife.

## References / links

-   [iNaturalist species page](https://www.inaturalist.org/taxa/129347-Ilex-glabra)
-   [Local profile](https://florida.plantatlas.usf.edu/plant/species/3793)
